text:
Somewhere in between trying to understand machine learning and trying to understand my own brain, I realized something slightly uncomfortable.

The problem isn’t that AI is too hard.
The problem is that I keep expecting to understand everything immediately.

Yesterday I spent 40 minutes learning one small concept and 30 minutes convincing myself that I’m not smart enough for this. Character development 👍

But slowly I’m starting to see a pattern. The people who get good at AI aren’t the smartest ones. They’re just the ones who survive confusion for a longer period of time.

So for now, the goal is simple:
Keep studying even when nothing makes sense.

And if something does make sense for 5 minutes, that’s already a win.

I used to think learning AI would feel exciting every day.

Reality:
– 10% excitement
– 20% curiosity
– 70% staring at something and pretending I understand it

Yesterday I was reading about overfitting and for a solid 15 minutes I was like: 'Yes yes this makes perfect sense.'
Then I tried explaining it in my own words and suddenly my brain logged out.

But I guess that’s the process. Understand → forget → understand again → slightly understand better → repeat.

Slow progress, but still progress.

One thing nobody tells beginners about learning AI is how much confidence goes up and down in a single day.

Morning: 'I think I can actually do this.'
Afternoon: 'Maybe this field is not for me.'
Night: 'Okay no, I’ll try again tomorrow.'

Somewhere in between all that, I did learn something small today. Not something impressive. Just something that made one concept slightly less scary than before.

And honestly, I think that’s how this journey actually works. Small improvements that nobody notices except you.

I always thought machine learning would be about algorithms, models, and complex math.

Turns out, most of the time it’s just:
Trying to understand what the dataset is even saying.

Today I spent more time figuring out the data than actually learning the model itself. And weirdly enough, that part was more interesting than the model.

So maybe the real skill in data science isn’t being a genius.
Maybe it’s just being patient enough to deal with messy data without losing motivation.

Still learning. Slowly, but consistently.

Today I realized something funny.

AI can write code in seconds.
But I still take 10 minutes deciding where to start.

At first that felt a bit discouraging. Like what’s even the point of learning if AI already knows everything faster?

But then it hit me. AI doesn’t replace thinking. It just exposes how clearly you can think.

If the idea is clear → the result is clear.
If the idea is messy → the result is messy.

So maybe the real skill I need to improve isn’t coding speed. It’s thinking clearly before I start.

Sometimes learning AI feels less like studying and more like surviving confusion.

You read something → understand 30% → forget 20% → question your life choices → continue anyway.

But the interesting part is this:
Every week something that looked impossible suddenly looks normal.

Things that scared me one month ago don’t feel that scary now. Not because I became super smart overnight, just because I kept showing up.

So for now, consistency > intelligence.

I used to believe that the hardest part of learning AI would be the math.

Turns out, the hardest part is staying consistent when progress feels invisible.

Some days you study for an hour and nothing feels different. No big achievement, no exciting result, just one small concept that makes slightly more sense than before.

But I guess that’s the game. Slow progress that only makes sense when you look back after a few weeks.

So for now, just trying to focus on improvement instead of speed.

One of the weirdest things about learning AI is how often you feel like you’re behind everyone else.

You open LinkedIn and suddenly everyone is building something insane while you’re still trying to understand the basics properly.

But then I realized something:
Nobody posts the confusion part. Only the results.

So instead of worrying about being behind, I’m trying to focus on understanding things properly, even if it takes longer.

Slow learning is still learning.

Today I had one of those small wins that look useless from the outside but feel huge personally.

Something that confused me for days suddenly made sense in 10 minutes.

No big achievement. No certificate. No project. Just understanding something that used to look impossible.

And honestly, those small moments are probably the real motivation behind learning AI. Not the big milestones, but those tiny breakthroughs that remind you that progress is actually happening.

I think one of the biggest misconceptions about learning AI is that you need to be extremely smart.

What I’m starting to realize instead is this:
You just need patience.

Patience to read something multiple times.
Patience to feel confused without quitting.
Patience to improve slowly without comparing yourself to everyone else.

Still at the beginning of this journey, but one thing is clear already. This isn’t a race. It’s more like a long-term consistency test.

And for now, just trying to pass that test one day at a time.

Today I spent more time trying to understand a concept than actually learning it.

I read it once → confused
Read it again → still confused
Watched a video → slightly less confused

And somehow after all that, one small part finally made sense.

Not the whole topic. Just one small part.

But I guess that’s how learning AI really works. Not one big moment of understanding. Just a lot of small ones stacked together.

I think beginners in AI have one hidden advantage.

We don’t pretend to understand things we actually don’t understand.

Every time I read something confusing, my brain just refuses to move forward until it makes at least a little sense.

It’s slow. Sometimes frustrating. But maybe that’s actually better than rushing through everything and forgetting it later.

So for now, slow learning > fake confidence.

Some days learning AI feels exciting.
Other days it feels like I’m just collecting confusing terms.

Overfitting
Underfitting
Feature engineering
Gradient descent

Sometimes it feels like I’m learning a new language instead of a new skill.

But slowly the words are starting to feel less scary. Still confusing, but less scary than before.

One thing I didn’t expect when starting AI learning was how often I would question myself.

Not the topic. Not the subject. Just myself.

'Am I learning too slowly?'
'Am I doing this the wrong way?'
'Am I wasting time?'

But somehow every week something small improves. Even if it’s not visible to anyone else.

So I guess the real progress isn’t just in learning AI. It’s also in learning patience.

I used to think progress in AI would look impressive.

Like finishing big projects, building complex models, doing something that looks smart from the outside.

But right now progress looks very different.

Understanding one small concept.
Fixing one small mistake.
Learning one thing that confused me yesterday.

Not impressive, but definitely real.
